title: Te Arawa

infobox.value: is a confederation of [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") and [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") (tribes and sub-tribes) of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") who trace their ancestry to the *[Arawa](/wiki/Arawa_(canoe) "Arawa (canoe)")* migration canoe (*waka*).[[1]](#cite_note-ara-1) The tribes are based in the [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") and [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") areas and have a population of around 60,117 according to the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), making the confederation the sixth biggest iwi in New Zealand.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Te Arawa iwi comprises 56 [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") (sub-tribes) and 31 [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae") (family groupings).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

["Demographics"](https://web.archive.org/web/20230201110644/https://tewhata.io/social/people/demographics/). *Te Whata*. Archived from [the original](https://tewhata.io/social/people/demographics/) on 1 February 2023. Retrieved 1 February 2023.
["About"](https://tearawa.io/about/). *Te Arawa Lakes Trust*. 21 December 2020. Retrieved 1 February 2023.

Tapsell, Paora (1 March 2017). ["Te Arawa – Settlement and migration"](https://teara.govt.nz/en/te-arawa/page-2). *[Te Ara – the Encyclopedia of New Zealand](/wiki/Te_Ara:_The_Encyclopedia_of_New_Zealand "Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand")*. Retrieved 1 November 2024.

["Deed of Settlement Summary"](https://www.tearawhiti.govt.nz/assets/Treaty-Settlements/FIND_Treaty_Settlements/Te-Arawa-Lakes/DOS_documents/Te-Arawa-Lakes-Deed-of-Settlement-Summary-18-Dec-2004.pdf) (PDF). New Zealand Government. 18 December 2004. Retrieved 1 November 2024.

Stokes, Jon (30 September 2006). ["$200m treaty deal makes tribe 'a force'"](http://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=10403715). *[The New Zealand Herald](/wiki/The_New_Zealand_Herald "The New Zealand Herald")*. Retrieved 19 November 2011.

McLachlan, Leigh Marama (18 December 2015). ["Rotorua iwi and council in new partnership"](http://www.radionz.co.nz/news/te-manu-korihi/292527/rotorua-iwi-and-council-in-new-partnership). Te Ao Maori. Radio New Zealand. Retrieved 28 July 2016. "Local iwi are being given the right to vote in committee meetings at the Rotorua Lakes Council. [...] The council has entered into a new partnership with Te Arawa which will see iwi more involved in decision making. [...] Today, it launched a new iwi partnership board, Te Tatau o Te Arawa."

["Who we are & what we do"](https://web.archive.org/web/20161105062302/https://tearawaonline.com/about/). *Te Arawa Online*. Te Arawa Communications. Archived from [the original](https://tearawaonline.com/about/) on 5 November 2016. Retrieved 20 December 2024.
"Major transformation for Te Arawa iwi radio station". No. 14. Kia Hiwa Ra. September 1993. p. 8.
["Rotorua"](https://web.archive.org/web/20090415173756/http://www.theradiovault.net/rotorua.htm). *Welcome to the Radio Vault*. New Zealand: The Radio Vault

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Confederation of Māori iwi and hapū (tribes and sub-tribes)

This article is about a confederation of Māori tribes. For the ancient Māori canoe, see [Arawa (canoe)](/wiki/Arawa_(canoe) "Arawa (canoe)"). For the radio station, see [Te Arawa FM](/wiki/Te_Arawa_FM "Te Arawa FM").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/The_three_families_stayed_one_night_at_Maketu_as_the_guests_of_the_Arawa_tribe%2C_1966.jpg/250px-The_three_families_stayed_one_night_at_Maketu_as_the_guests_of_the_Arawa_tribe%2C_1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_three_families_stayed_one_night_at_Maketu_as_the_guests_of_the_Arawa_tribe,_1966.jpg)

The Te Arawa of [Maketu](/wiki/Maketu "Maketu") entertain guests.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/M%C4%81kereti_Papakura%2C_1908_-_%28unknown_photographer%29_%284585763974%29.jpg/250px-M%C4%81kereti_Papakura%2C_1908_-_%28unknown_photographer%29_%284585763974%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C4%81kereti_Papakura,_1908_-_(unknown_photographer)_(4585763974).jpg)

Maggie Papakura (1873–1930) was a guide, entertainer and ethnographer. Her mother was of the Ngāti Wāhiao hapu of the [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi") tribe of Te Arawa and her father was from England.

**Te Arawa** is a confederation of [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") and [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") (tribes and sub-tribes) of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") who trace their ancestry to the *[Arawa](/wiki/Arawa_(canoe) "Arawa (canoe)")* migration canoe (*waka*).[[1]](#cite_note-ara-1) The tribes are based in the [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") and [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") areas and have a population of around 60,117 according to the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), making the confederation the sixth biggest iwi in New Zealand.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Te Arawa iwi comprises 56 [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") (sub-tribes) and 31 [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae") (family groupings).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Te_Arawa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Tamatekapua%2C_Te_Papa-i-Ouru_Marae%2C_1975_%2828871450256%29.jpg/250px-Tamatekapua%2C_Te_Papa-i-Ouru_Marae%2C_1975_%2828871450256%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tamatekapua,_Te_Papa-i-Ouru_Marae,_1975_(28871450256).jpg)

Te Papaiouru marae at [Ohinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu"), [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua"), in 1975. It is the home marae of the [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue") subtribes Ngāti Tae-o-Tū and Ngāti Tūnohopū.

Te Arawa iwi are descended from people who migrated to New Zealand on the *Arawa* canoe. They settled in the [Bay of Plenty region](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Region "Bay of Plenty Region"), principally around the [Rotorua lakes](/wiki/Lakes_of_Rotorua "Lakes of Rotorua").[[1]](#cite_note-ara-1) Three main subtribes developed: [Ngāti Pikiao](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Pikiao "Ngāti Pikiao") occupied the eastern end of [Lake Rotoiti](/wiki/Lake_Rotoiti_(Bay_of_Plenty) "Lake Rotoiti (Bay of Plenty)") and the area around [Lake Rotoehu](/wiki/Lake_Rotoehu "Lake Rotoehu") and [Lake Rotomā](/wiki/Lake_Rotom%C4%81 "Lake Rotomā"); [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi") occupied the upper [Kaituna River](/wiki/Kaituna_River "Kaituna River"), western Lake Rotoiti and the south-east side of [Lake Rotorua](/wiki/Lake_Rotorua "Lake Rotorua") including [Ōhinemutu](/wiki/%C5%8Chinemutu "Ōhinemutu"); [Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue"), formerly known as Te Uri o Uenukukōpako, occupied [Mokoia Island](/wiki/Mokoia_Island "Mokoia Island") and the north-west side of Lake Rotorua.[[4]](#cite_note-Ara_Settle-4)

Many Te Arawa men fought for the colonial government in the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars") that took place in the North Island in the mid-19th century. Perhaps in part, for this reason, the iwi chose to negotiate directly with the [New Zealand Government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government") over their historical grievances, bypassing the [Waitangi Tribunal](/wiki/Waitangi_Tribunal "Waitangi Tribunal"). A series of negotiations has resulted in several settlements of their various claims, the largest of which involve the settlement relating to the 14 lakes, signed in December 2004,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and the settlement for all the historical claims of a cluster of Te Arawa iwi and hapu signed on 30 September 2006. The Government apologised to Te Arawa for breaches of the [Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") and paid $36 million in compensation, including up to 500 km² of Crown forest land, as well as 19 areas of special significance, including the Whakarewarewa Thermal Springs Reserve.[[6]](#cite_note-NZ_Herald_10403715-6)

On 18 December 2015 Te Arawa gained the right to vote in committee meetings of the Rotorua Lakes Council via an iwi partnership board, Te Tatau o Te Arawa.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Te Arawa FM](/wiki/Te_Arawa_FM "Te Arawa FM") is the radio station of Te Arawa iwi, including Ngāti Pikiao, Tūhourangi and Ngāti Whakaue. It was established in the early 1980s and became a charitable entity in November 1990.[[8]](#cite_note-tearawa-8) The station underwent a major transformation in 1993, becoming Whanau FM.[[9]](#cite_note-transformarawa-9) One of the station's frequencies 99.1 was taken over by [Mai FM](/wiki/Mai_FM "Mai FM") in 1998; the other became Pumanawa 89FM before later reverting to Te Arawa FM.[[10]](#cite_note-rotorua-10) It is available on 88.7 FM in [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") the 99.1 frequency is now broadcasting commercial station [The Heat 991 FM](/wiki/The_Heat_991_FM "The Heat 991 FM") which started broadcasting 15 April 2015.[[11]](#cite_note-maorimedia-11)

## Constituent iwi and hapu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Te_Arawa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Constituent iwi and hapu")]

The iwi and hapu that constitute Te Arawa include:

[Ngāti Whakaue](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Whakaue "Ngāti Whakaue"), [Ngāti Rangiteaorere](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Rangiteaorere "Ngāti Rangiteaorere"), [Ngāti Pikiao](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Pikiao "Ngāti Pikiao"), [Ngāti Mākino](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_M%C4%81kino "Ngāti Mākino"), [Ngāti Rangitihi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Rangitihi "Ngāti Rangitihi"), [Ngāti Rangiwewehi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Rangiwewehi "Ngāti Rangiwewehi"), [Tapuika](/wiki/Tapuika "Tapuika"), [Waitaha](/wiki/Waitaha_(Bay_of_Plenty_iwi) "Waitaha (Bay of Plenty iwi)"), [Ngāti Ngararanui](/w/index.php?title=Ng%C4%81ti_Ngararanui&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ngāti Ngararanui (page does not exist)"), [Ngāti Rongomai](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Rongomai "Ngāti Rongomai"), [Ngāti Tahu – Ngāti Whaoa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Tahu_%E2%80%93_Ng%C4%81ti_Whaoa "Ngāti Tahu – Ngāti Whaoa"), [Ngāti Tarāwhai](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Tar%C4%81whai "Ngāti Tarāwhai"), [Ngāti Te Roro o Te Rangi](/w/index.php?title=Ng%C4%81ti_Te_Roro_o_Te_Rangi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ngāti Te Roro o Te Rangi (page does not exist)"), [Ngāti Kea Ngāti Tuara](/w/index.php?title=Ng%C4%81ti_Kea_Ng%C4%81ti_Tuara&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ngāti Kea Ngāti Tuara (page does not exist)"), [Ngāti Tura-Ngāti Te Ngakau](/w/index.php?title=Ng%C4%81ti_Tura-Ng%C4%81ti_Te_Ngakau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ngāti Tura-Ngāti Te Ngakau (page does not exist)"), [Ngāti Uenukukōpako](/wiki/Uenuku-K%C5%8Dpako "Uenuku-Kōpako"), [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi"), [Ngāti Hei](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Hei "Ngāti Hei"), [Ngāti Huarere](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Huarere "Ngāti Huarere") and [Ngāti Wāhiao](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_W%C4%81hiao "Ngāti Wāhiao").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Te_Arawa&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable people")]

* [Manu Bennett](/wiki/Manu_Bennett "Manu Bennett"), Actor
* [Tāmati Coffey](/wiki/T%C4%81mati_Coffey "Tāmati Coffey"), Presenter and former MP
* [Cliff Curtis](/wiki/Cliff_Curtis "Cliff Curtis"), Actor
* [Kepa Ehau](/wiki/Kepa_Hamuera_Anaha_Ehau "Kepa Hamuera Anaha Ehau"), Māori leader and nationally acclaimed orator
* [Te Ururoa Flavell](/wiki/Te_Ururoa_Flavell "Te Ururoa Flavell"), Former minister and co-leader of TPM
* [Gillies Kaka](/wiki/Gillies_Kaka "Gillies Kaka"), Olympian and NZ rugby union player
* [Troy Kingi](/wiki/Troy_Kingi "Troy Kingi"), Artist
* Sir [Howard Morrison](/wiki/Howard_Morrison "Howard Morrison"), Entertainer
* [Scotty Morrison](/wiki/Scotty_Morrison_(broadcaster) "Scotty Morrison (broadcaster)"), Writer and broadcaster
* [Stacey Morrison](/wiki/Stacey_Morrison "Stacey Morrison"), TV host and MC
* [Temuera Morrison](/wiki/Temuera_Morrison "Temuera Morrison"), Actor
* [Mākereti Papakura](/wiki/M%C4%81kereti_Papakura "Mākereti Papakura"), Tourist guide and ethnographer
* [TJ Perenara](/wiki/TJ_Perenara "TJ Perenara"), NZ rugby union player
* [Maisey Rika](/wiki/Maisey_Rika "Maisey Rika"), Artist
* [Mita Taupopoki](/wiki/Mita_Taupopoki "Mita Taupopoki"), Māori leader and orator

## External links
